text: Last class you gave a status update to headquarters.
Today the client is hesitant, and you have to persuade them.
Persuade
Hesitant
Incentive
Trial
Guarantee
Worthwhile
Convince
Win-win
I understand your hesitation. Let me make you an offer.
What if we found a win-win solution?
Let me give you an incentive to decide today.
If we lowered the price, you would save a lot.
If you ordered more, we would offer a discount.
If you signed today, we would guarantee delivery.
If we added a free trial, it would be a win-win.
We really like the engines, Roberto. But honestly, we are hesitant about the price. It is a little high for us.
I understand your concern. Let me persuade you with a better deal. If you ordered 500 units, we would offer a five percent discount.
That is interesting. But we also worry about delivery times. We need the engines by March.
No problem. If you signed the contract today, we would guarantee delivery in March. And if you wanted, we would add a free trial of the new model.
A free trial? Now that sounds worthwhile. That would really be a win-win for both of us.
Exactly. The trial is a strong incentive, and the guarantee removes the risk. If we did this together, I am sure it would convince your board.
If you ordered more, we would offer a better discount.
A free trial would be a strong incentive for you.
This way, the deal would be a win-win for both of us.

Hi Roberto, this is Amy. I am calling to brief you before your call with the client this afternoon. Here is the situation. They love the engines, but they are hesitant about the price. The deal is not lost, but they need a reason to commit today. So this is a moment to persuade, not to push. The best tool here is the second conditional. You imagine a result that depends on a condition. For example, you could say: if we lowered the price by five percent, you would save a lot on a large order. Or: if you signed the contract today, we would guarantee delivery in March. Notice the shape. In the if part, use the past simple, lowered, signed, ordered. In the result part, use would plus the base verb, you would save, we would guarantee. It sounds polite and professional, because you are not demanding anything. You are offering a worthwhile trade. Try to make it a win-win. Offer a small incentive, like a free trial or a faster delivery, in exchange for a bigger commitment. Stay calm, sound confident, and let the conditional do the work. Good luck on the call, and tell me how it goes.

This is a quick summary of my call with the client this afternoon. They were still hesitant about the price, so I did not push. Instead, I used the second conditional to offer a few hypothetical deals. I told them that if they ordered five hundred units, we would give them a better discount. I also said that if they signed before Friday, we would guarantee delivery in March and add a free trial of the new model. They liked the idea of a win-win. The free trial was a strong incentive, and the delivery guarantee removed their main concern. In the end, I think I managed to persuade them. They said they would discuss it internally and confirm by Friday. It felt natural to use would and the past simple to make the offers, and the client never felt pushed. I will send Amy the full notes this evening, and I am confident this is going to close.

First, listen carefully and understand why the client is hesitant. Then, acknowledge their concern so they feel heard. After that, offer a hypothetical deal using the second conditional, if you ordered more, we would lower the price. Next, add a small incentive to make it a win-win. Finally, confirm the next step and let them think it over.
